children.name: Falco
children.description: The archetypal birds of prey — streamlined, pointed-winged, lightning-fast. The tomial tooth on the upper mandible severs prey's spinal cord on impact. Five species breed in Sweden regularly, from the hovering kestrel of roadsides to the peregrine reclaiming Stockholm's tower blocks.

children.name: Falco tinnunculus
children.description: Sweden's familiar hovering falcon, hanging motionless on rapidly beating wings above roadside verges and field margins as it scans for voles. It can see ultraviolet light, allowing it to detect the urine trails of small rodents.

children.name: Falco columbarius
children.description: The smallest European falcon, a compact dashing hunter that pursues small birds in low fast flight across open country. Breeds on Swedish moorland and bogs and disperses to coasts and farmland in winter.

children.name: Falco subbuteo
children.description: A dashing, scythe-winged summer migrant that hawks dragonflies and hirundines in spectacular aerial pursuits, often over wetlands at dusk. It catches and eats much of its insect prey on the wing.

children.name: Falco peregrinus
children.description: The fastest animal on Earth, reaching over 300 km/h in a hunting stoop on flying prey. After near-extinction from DDT, it has recovered strongly and now nests on cliffs, quarries, and city towers across Sweden.

children.name: Falco rusticolus
children.description: The largest falcon in the world, a powerful predator of the Swedish mountain tundra where it hunts ptarmigan and waterfowl in a fast, level chase rather than a stoop. Birds range from near-white to dark grey-brown.

children.name: Falco vespertinus
children.description: A graceful, sociable little falcon that breeds colonially in eastern Europe and appears in Sweden as a scarce but regular spring overshoot. Males are slate-grey with rust-red 'trousers' and red feet.
